text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac and exposes the insertion of the gastrocolic trunk near the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The anterior surface of the duodenum is being exposed near the hepatic flexure and gallbladder.
The surgeon employs a trans mesenteric medial approach to access the duodenum, revealing two GIST tumors visible through the mesentery.
The duodenum and the GIST tumor are clearly visible through the mesentery. The surgeon exposes the anterior surface of the pancreatic head and the duodenum.

The surgeon separates the pancreatic head from the mesentery of the colon and mobilizes the remainder of the duodenum. The patient is positioned similarly to a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy. A full Kocher maneuver is performed, revealing the inferior vena cava posterior to the pancreas and duodenum.

The inferior vena cava (IVC) pulsations are visible, indicating its proximity to the surgical field. The duodenum exhibits excellent mobility, suggesting it can be easily manipulated. The specimen appears ready to be wedged out after excision and placed in a bag. The view provides a clear perspective for suturing the duodenum closed.

The surgeon employs a two-layer running suture technique to close the duodenotomy, with the bowel occluded distal to the closure. An underwater test is conducted using a gastroscope, showing no leakage and good distention of the duodenum.

The surgeon approaches the duodenum with a trans mesenteric medial technique, navigating through a mesentery with increased fatty tissue due to mesenteric obesity. The gastrointestinal stromal tumor is visible, and the patient is positioned similarly to a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon tents up the iliocolic pedicle and the right colic, or possibly the right branch of the middle colic, to create a trans mesenteric window. A flap is raised, exposing the duodenum and the tumor. The pancreatic head is visible, and the duodenum is fully mobilized.

The surgeon performs a full-thickness excision of the duodenal wall, followed by a two-layer closure technique. The duodenum is observed peristalsing, and an underwater test with a gastroscope confirms no leakage.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the duodenum using a trans mesenteric medial approach, with the duodenum visible coming through the mesentery. Adhesions tacking up the cecum are addressed, and the patient is positioned in Trendelenburg to enhance visibility.
The surgeon mobilizes the anterior surface of the duodenum and pancreatic head, achieving clear visualization of the pancreatic head and the remainder of the duodenum.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral and posterior attachments of the duodenum, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) in the surgical field. A window is created to the left side of the mesentery, and the ligament of Treitz is opened, providing clear visualization of the IVC.

The surgeon demonstrates mobility of the duodenum and the fourth part of the duodenum along with the proximal jejunum, bringing them into the trans mesenteric window. Due to insufficient laxity, the structures are returned, and further mobilization is performed.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the duodenum, revealing the root of the superior mesenteric artery off the aorta. The proximal duodenum is visible, and attachments of the ligament of trites and mesentery are addressed. The proximal jejunum and distal duodenum are suitably mobilized, allowing for division of the bowel using a laparoscopic stapler.
The surgeon flips the extensively mobilized duodenum into the right upper quadrant, demonstrating ease and comfort with the trans mesenteric medial approach.

The surgeon reflects the duodenum medially, using cautery to carefully expose its anterior surface. The posterior and lateral attachments are being taken, revealing the inferior vena cava and the left branch of the renal vein. The superior mesenteric artery's origin is visualized, along with the pancreatic head and anterior surface of the duodenum. The transmesenteric mobilization allows full visualization of the porta hepatis and gallbladder.
